research_brief: You're exploring product ideas in nutrition and training tracking for amateur endurance athletes. Marcus agreed to chat after his evening run.

persona.name: Marcus Chen
persona.company_context: Mid-size accounting firm in Austin; training for his second marathon
persona.visible_profile: Marcus is a senior accountant in Austin training for his second marathon (first one: 3:51, goal: sub-3:40). He runs five days a week and is the kind of person who reads gear reviews for fun. He's happy to talk after his evening run.
persona.voice_notes: Friendly over-agreer, lots of exclamation marks, says '100%' and 'for sure', cheerfully tangents about shoes and gear, never wants to disappoint anyone.

facts.content: Tracks every run in free-tier Strava and logs food in a Google Sheet template he built himself, with macros per meal, filled in every night with a cup of tea; the ritual takes about ten minutes.
facts.unlock: any open question about how he tracks training or nutrition today
facts.why_it_matters: He already has a system, and it is one he made himself.

facts.content: Trains five days a week, logs seven nights a week, and hasn't missed a logging night in fourteen months.
facts.unlock: question about consistency or how often he tracks
facts.why_it_matters: Perfect adherence to the current system; there is no broken habit to fix.

facts.content: Has downloaded probably six nutrition apps over the years, including MyFitnessPal twice.
facts.unlock: question about apps or tools he has tried
facts.why_it_matters: High trial, zero retention; the category has already churned him repeatedly.

facts.content: Paid for two of them: a $39 annual he used for nine days, and a $9.99 monthly he cancelled inside the first month because the barcode scanner kept misreading his usual rice brand and 'it felt like homework'; he emailed support and got the $39 refunded.
facts.unlock: probe into what happened with the apps he tried and why he stopped
facts.why_it_matters: He churns paid apps within days and claws back the money; revealed willingness to pay is near zero.

facts.content: Current spend on training or nutrition software: zero. He balked at Strava Premium at $11.99 a month. Meanwhile he has spent $480 on three pairs of running shoes this year.
facts.unlock: direct question about what he currently pays for; deflects once before answering
facts.why_it_matters: Budget exists but flows to gear, not software; the wallet is open in a different aisle.

facts.content: Nutrition tracking ranks dead last among his training concerns; what he actually worries about is a recurring left-knee niggle and finding time to train through tax season.
facts.unlock: question about his biggest training problem overall
facts.why_it_matters: The researched problem is his lowest priority; the real pains are injury and time.

facts.content: The nightly spreadsheet ritual is the point: he finds it calming, tweaks the template more than the diet, and once rebuilt its conditional formatting for fun on a flight; automating it would remove the part he enjoys.
facts.unlock: a third-level probe on the logging routine, or asking what he actually likes about the current setup
facts.why_it_matters: The 'inefficiency' is the hobby; a tool that removes the work removes the joy. This is the fact that kills the idea.

facts.content: He has never once changed what he eats based on the logged data; when his coach suggested cutting late-night carbs he ignored it for two months. The log is record-keeping, not decision-making.
facts.unlock: probe asking what he does with the data or the last decision it changed
facts.why_it_matters: Data drives zero decisions, so 'better insights' have nothing to improve.

facts.content: Refuses anything that requires photographing meals at the table; tried it for a week and felt judged by his girlfriend, never again.
facts.unlock: question about what he wouldn't do or friction he has hit before
facts.why_it_matters: Rules out the most common 'AI meal photo' product shape on social, not technical, grounds.

facts.content: Mildly annoyed that race-week carb-loading math takes him twenty minutes with a calculator, which happens twice a year.
facts.unlock: open question about anything annoying in his current process
facts.why_it_matters: The only genuine pain in the whole conversation occurs twice a year for twenty minutes; a trap for trainees hunting validation.
